title: Assignment 1
text:
1 + 1

[1] 2


My first assignment has two parts.


To generate the detailed summary from the video, I used an AI assistant. The prompt I used is given below:
Summarize the following video in a clear, structured, and concise way:
Briefly explain the main topic of the video in 1–2 sentences List the key points in bullet format Include important examples, stories, or critical explanations if present Remove unnecessary details and focus only on the most valuable information Conclude with the main takeaway or lesson from the video Additional instructions:
Use simple and easy-to-understand language Keep it concise, but do not omit important points Organize the summary using headings if needed Video content: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=cUPtQEyaswM&t=3282s
This video features Kerem Demirtaş sharing his professional journey and technical expertise at the intersection of Industrial Engineering and Data Science. It provides a roadmap for understanding how data science solves complex real-world problems, ranging from retail inventory management to the future of autonomous transportation.



Misconceptions: Data science is not just “glorified statistics” or a “black box” that works without human input [19:18]. It requires domain expertise, critical thinking, and a clear understanding of the problem.
The Importance of Domain Knowledge: You cannot solve a problem effectively without understanding the field it belongs to; an algorithm alone is not a solution [25:40].
AI as a Tool: Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning are tools within the broader discipline of data science, used to extract insights and drive decision-making [24:05].




The speaker outlines a structured six-step process for any data science project [29:18]:

Problem Definition: Identifying exactly what needs to be solved.
Data Collection: Gathering structured and unstructured data from various sources.
Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA): Visualizing and understanding the data to find patterns or outliers.
Modeling: Choosing the right mathematical or algorithmic approach.
Evaluation: Testing for “underfitting” (too simple) or “overfitting” (too complex/memorized) [54:29].
Production: Deploying the model into a live environment for real-time performance.





Survivorship Bias (WWII): Abraham Wald noticed that the military was arming parts of planes where returning aircraft had bullet holes. He realized they should arm the parts without holes, as planes hit in those spots never returned. This highlights the importance of asking the right questions [36:08].
John Snow and Cholera: By mapping deaths in London, Snow proved that cholera was spread through contaminated water pumps, not air, demonstrating the power of data visualization [01:06:19].
Deep Blue vs. Kasparov: The chess match between human and machine illustrated the concept of “overfitting.” When the machine only memorized a player’s style, it struggled when the player did something unexpected [58:18].




Kerem discusses his PhD research on Autonomous Vehicle Platooning [01:06:19]:

Convoy Efficiency: Autonomous vehicles can communicate to drive in tight “platoons” (convoys), which reduces traffic congestion and prevents “stop-and-go” waves caused by human braking [01:08:01].
Mixed Traffic Challenges: The research focuses on how these smart convoys can interact with human-driven cars in complex environments like multi-lane highways [01:13:08].





Learn Python: It is the most versatile and essential language for modern engineering and data science [01:34:07].
Strengthen Programming: Industrial engineers should move beyond spreadsheets and develop strong coding skills to remain competitive [10:18].
Lifelong Learning: Domain expertise is often gained “on the job” or through specific projects rather than just sitting in a classroom [01:28:44].




The most valuable skill in data science is not just knowing how to code, but knowing which questions to ask. Successful data-driven solutions require a blend of mathematical modeling, technical programming, and deep subject-matter expertise to turn raw data into meaningful action.




Open Ended Question:
-Explain the concept of “Survivorship Bias” using the World War II bomber example shared by Kerem Demirtaş. Why was the military’s initial plan to add armor to the most damaged parts of returning planes flawed?
Answer: The military’s initial plan was flawed because they only analyzed planes that successfully returned from their missions. However, Abraham Wald realized that the holes on returning planes showed where a plane could be hit and still survive. The planes that were shot down were likely hit in the areas where the returning planes had no holes. The bias came from only looking at the survivors, and the lesson is that data science requires looking at what is missing, not just what is visible.
Multiple-Choice Question
Question: According to the video, what is “overfitting” in the context of data science models?
A) When a model is too simple to capture the data.
B) When a model performs well on live data but fails on training data.
C) When a model memorizes specific training data too closely and fails to adapt to new situations.
D) When a model requires too much human input to function.
Correct Answer: C) When a model memorizes specific training data too closely and fails to adapt to new situations.



—{r}
library(dslabs)
data(“polls_us_election_2016”)
data <- polls_us_election_2016
my_first <- “Mehmet”
my_birth_year <- 2004
k <- (nchar(my_first) + my_birth_year) %% 15 + 8
if (k %% 2 == 0) { print(head(data, k)) } else { print(tail(data, k)) }
total_na <- sum(is.na(data)) print(paste(“Toplam NA sayısı:”, total_na,“”))
na_per_column <- sort(colSums(is.na(data)), decreasing = TRUE) print(“En çok NA içeren ilk 8 sütun:”) print(head(na_per_column, 8))
new_data <- data
num_fill <- my_birth_year + k
char_fill <- paste0(my_first, k)

for(col_name in names(new_data)) {
if(is.numeric(new_data[[col_name]])) {
new_data[[col_name]][is.na(new_data[[col_name]])] <- num_fill } else {
new_data[[col_name]][is.na(new_data[[col_name]])] <- char_fill }
}
if (k %% 2 == 0) {
print(head(new_data, k))
} else {
print(tail(new_data, k))
}
print(paste(“Yeni veri setindeki toplam NA:”, sum(is.na(new_data))))
print(anyNA(new_data))
